eli3: Causal (autoregressive) language modeling predicts the next token given all previous tokens, strictly left-to-right. 'Causal' means each token can only attend to earlier tokens — no peeking at future context. This is the training objective of GPT, Llama, and all major text-generation LLMs. The model learns P(wₜ | w₁,…,wₜ₋₁) for every position simultaneously via teacher forcing.

The key mechanic: a causal attention mask blocks the upper triangle of the attention matrix (setting those attention scores to −∞ before softmax). This ensures position 3 only attends to positions 1, 2, 3 — never 4, 5, 6, etc. At training time, all positions are computed in parallel (unlike RNNs). At inference, tokens are generated one at a time, each attending to all previously generated tokens.

Causal LMs are the backbone of text generation, instruction following, and in-context learning. Once scaled up (GPT-3, GPT-4, Llama 2), they exhibit emergent abilities: few-shot learning, chain-of-thought reasoning, code generation. The same model architecture that predicts the next token can be fine-tuned (SFT) and aligned (RLHF) to become a conversational assistant.

whenWhy.title: Text generation and completion
whenWhy.body: Causal LMs generate text by autoregressive sampling: sample from P(wₜ|w₁,…,wₜ₋₁) at each step. Applications: story completion, code generation (GitHub Copilot uses Codex, a GPT model fine-tuned on code), document drafting, dialogue. Causal LMs can be prompted in-context: prepend examples, and the model continues the pattern. This enables few-shot classification, translation, and summarization without fine-tuning.

whenWhy.title: In-context learning and instruction following
whenWhy.body: Causal LMs support in-context learning (ICL): providing a few examples in the prompt allows the model to perform new tasks without gradient updates. GPT-3 demonstrated ICL at scale. Instruction fine-tuning (InstructGPT, FLAN): fine-tune causal LMs on (instruction, output) pairs — model learns to follow natural language instructions. RLHF further aligns the model with human preferences. All major LLM assistants (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) use causal LM + SFT + RLHF.

whenWhy.title: Prefix LM and encoder-decoder variants
whenWhy.body: Causal LMs can be adapted: prefix LM uses bidirectional attention on the prompt (prefix) and causal attention on the generation (suffix) — allows richer prompt encoding while maintaining generation capability. T5 uses an encoder-decoder: encoder processes input bidirectionally, decoder generates output causally. Encoder-decoder models (T5, BART) excel at seq2seq tasks (summarization, translation); pure causal LMs (GPT, Llama) are more flexible for generative tasks and in-context learning.

deepDiveIntro: The training objective is to maximise the likelihood of the actual next token at every position — equivalently, minimise the average cross-entropy loss across all token positions. During training, ground truth tokens are used as context for predicting each next token (teacher forcing), which allows all positions to be computed in parallel rather than one at a time. At inference time, the model must generate tokens sequentially, using its own previous predictions as context.

The causal attention mask is what makes this work: before computing attention weights, every position in the upper triangle of the attention matrix (positions that represent future tokens) is set to negative infinity so they receive zero weight after softmax. This is the only architectural difference between GPT-style models and BERT — the same building blocks, just different masking. At inference, a key-value cache stores computed representations for all previous tokens, so each new token only needs to attend to that cache rather than recomputing everything from scratch.

deepDive.heading: Causal Attention Mechanism
deepDive.body: The attention scores are computed by comparing each position's query to every key. Before taking the softmax, positions that correspond to future tokens are masked to negative infinity, so they get zero weight — this enforces the left-to-right constraint. All positions in the sequence are processed in parallel during training (unlike an RNN that must step through the sequence), yet the mask ensures each position can only see past tokens. The same model and weights handle both parallel training and sequential generation, which is what makes transformers so efficient. [1]
deepDive.formula: A[i,j] = softmax((QKᵀ/√d + M)[i,j]);  M[i,j] = −∞ if j > i else 0

deepDive.heading: KV-Cache for Efficient Inference
deepDive.body: Without caching, generating each new token would require recomputing the key and value representations for every token in the context, which gets expensive fast. The KV-cache solves this by storing those representations after the first computation and reusing them at every subsequent step. The cost is memory: for very long contexts (128K tokens), the cache alone can require tens of gigabytes. Quantising the cache values to lower precision (8-bit or 4-bit integers) reduces memory without much quality loss. [1]

deepDive.heading: Decoding Strategies
deepDive.body: Greedy decoding always picks the single most likely next token — fast but tends to produce repetitive text. Beam search maintains several candidate sequences and picks the best one at the end — good for machine translation but unnatural for open-ended chat. Sampling draws from the probability distribution, with top-k sampling restricting to the k most likely tokens and nucleus (top-p) sampling restricting to the smallest set of tokens whose combined probability reaches a threshold p. Temperature scales the distribution: lower temperature makes the model more confident and deterministic, higher temperature makes it more creative and random. A default of temperature 0.7 and top-p 0.9 works well for most dialogue applications. [1]

deepDive.heading: Causal LM vs. Masked LM: When to Use Which
deepDive.body: Causal LMs (GPT, Llama) are the right choice for text generation, few-shot prompting, and instruction following — they scale well and can handle diverse tasks without task-specific fine-tuning. Masked LMs (BERT, RoBERTa) were the standard for NLU tasks like classification and named entity recognition where you have labelled data and need strong representations, but they cannot generate text. At large enough scales, causal LMs fine-tuned with human feedback now match or exceed BERT on understanding tasks, so the modern trend is to unify generation and understanding in a single causal model. [1]

takeaways: Causal LM: predict wₜ from w₁…wₜ₋₁ via causal attention mask (upper triangle = −∞). [1]
Teacher forcing: ground truth tokens as context during training — all positions computed in parallel. [1]
KV-cache: store K,V for previous tokens — reduces per-step inference cost from O(t²) to O(t). [1]
Nucleus sampling (top-p): best decoding for open-ended generation; temperature controls diversity. [1]
Causal LMs + SFT + RLHF = modern LLM assistants (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini). [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the difference between causal LM and seq2seq (encoder-decoder) for text generation?
edgeCases.a: Causal LM: single transformer stack — input and output share the same sequence. Prefix (input) tokens are generated causally. The model 'generates' the output by continuing the input prefix. Seq2seq (T5, BART): separate encoder (bidirectional) processes input; decoder (causal) generates output attending to encoder representations via cross-attention. Encoder-decoder advantages: (1) longer effective context (encoder sees full input, decoder generates output independently); (2) bidirectional encoding of input — better for structured tasks like summarization and translation. Causal LM advantages: simpler architecture, scales to larger sizes, supports in-context learning naturally, no separate encode/decode step. [1]

edgeCases.q: How does teacher forcing cause exposure bias, and how is it addressed?
edgeCases.a: Teacher forcing: during training, ground truth wₜ₋₁ is fed as context for predicting wₜ — regardless of what the model would have predicted. At inference, model prediction ŵₜ₋₁ is used as context for step t. If ŵₜ₋₁ ≠ wₜ₋₁ (error), the error propagates. This train/test distribution mismatch is called exposure bias. Mitigations: (1) Scheduled sampling (Bengio et al. 2015) — gradually replace teacher-forced tokens with model predictions during training; (2) Minimum Risk Training — optimize sequence-level reward (BLEU) rather than token-level cross-entropy; (3) RLHF — sequence-level reward optimization from human preferences. In practice, large LMs with teacher forcing work well enough that exposure bias is a minor issue. [1]

sources.label: Vaswani et al. (2017) — Attention Is All You Need, NeurIPS 2017
